mixed.question: Which planet in our solar system has the shortest day, completing one rotation in just under 10 hours?
mixed.fact: Despite being the largest planet, Jupiter spins so fast it visibly bulges at its equator by 7%.

mixed.question: In 2025, NASA's James Webb Space Telescope detected possible signs of dimethyl sulfide in the atmosphere of which exoplanet?
mixed.fact: On Earth, dimethyl sulfide is produced almost exclusively by living organisms, mostly marine phytoplankton.

mixed.question: What happens to the volume of space debris if a single satellite is destroyed in orbit, due to the Kessler Syndrome cascade effect?
options: It decreases as fragments burn up
mixed.fact: A single 2007 Chinese anti-satellite test created over 3,500 pieces of trackable debris still orbiting today.

mixed.question: True or False: The Great Wall of China is visible from the Moon with the naked eye.
options: False — but it's visible from low Earth orbit
False — it's never been visible from space at all
False — it's not even visible from low Earth orbit without aid
mixed.fact: The Wall is only about 6 meters wide — far too narrow to see from orbit, let alone the Moon.

mixed.question: Who holds the world record for the longest time spent in space across multiple missions, surpassing 878 days in total?
mixed.fact: Russian cosmonaut Oleg Kononenko broke the all-time cumulative spaceflight record in 2024 aboard the ISS.

mixed.question: What is the world record for the deepest scuba dive ever achieved, set by Ahmed Gabr in 2014?
mixed.fact: The dive took only 12 minutes to descend but nearly 14 hours of decompression stops to resurface safely.

mixed.question: Which person holds the record for surviving the highest G-force ever recorded in a non-fatal crash — 214 Gs?
mixed.fact: David Purley survived a deceleration from 173 km/h to zero in just 66 centimeters at a 1977 race.

mixed.question: Which two U.S. presidents both died on July 4, 1826 — exactly 50 years after the Declaration of Independence?
mixed.fact: John Adams's last words were reportedly 'Thomas Jefferson survives' — not knowing Jefferson had died hours earlier.

mixed.question: Which famous author was born in 1835 when Halley's Comet appeared and died in 1910 when it returned, as he had predicted?
mixed.fact: Twain said: 'I came in with Halley's Comet and expect to go out with it.' He died one day after its closest approach.

mixed.question: In a bizarre historic coincidence, King Umberto I of Italy met a restaurant owner who shared his name, birthday, and wedding date — what happened to both men on the same day?
mixed.fact: The restaurant owner was shot dead that same morning, and King Umberto I was assassinated later that day in 1900.

science.question: What happens to the speed of light when it passes through a Bose-Einstein condensate — the coldest known state of matter?
options: It slows to about half speed
It can slow to walking pace or even stop
It speeds up slightly due to quantum effects
science.fact: In 1999, scientists slowed light to just 17 meters per second through a Bose-Einstein condensate — slower than a bicycle.

science.question: Which bizarre quantum phenomenon allows particles to tunnel through barriers they classically shouldn't have enough energy to cross?
science.fact: Without quantum tunneling, the Sun wouldn't shine — its core isn't hot enough for fusion by classical physics alone.

science.question: True or False: In a vacuum, a heavier object falls faster than a lighter one due to its greater gravitational pull on Earth.
options: True — heavier objects technically accelerate Earth toward them faster
True — Newton's second law demands it
False — all objects fall at exactly the same rate regardless
False — lighter objects actually fall faster due to buoyancy
science.fact: Technically, a heavier object pulls Earth toward it slightly more, making the mutual closing speed imperceptibly faster.

science.question: In 2025, researchers at the National Ignition Facility achieved fusion ignition again. What fundamental force must be overcome to fuse two hydrogen nuclei?
science.fact: Positively charged nuclei violently repel each other — overcoming this Coulomb barrier requires temperatures exceeding 100 million degrees.

science.question: Which marine creature has three hearts, blue blood, and the ability to edit its own RNA on the fly to adapt to temperature changes?
science.fact: Octopuses can recode their neural proteins by editing messenger RNA, bypassing DNA — an ability almost no other animal has.

science.question: What is the name of the massive underwater sound channel through which whale calls can travel thousands of kilometers?
science.fact: The SOFAR channel at ~1,000m depth traps sound waves so efficiently that explosions were once detected 25,000 km away.

science.question: Which species of fish can survive being frozen in Arctic sea ice and resume normal activity after thawing?
science.fact: Arctic cod produce antifreeze glycoproteins that prevent ice crystals from growing inside their cells during extreme cold.

science.question: How many neurons does the human gut's enteric nervous system contain, earning it the nickname 'the second brain'?
science.fact: Your gut has roughly 500 million neurons — more than a cat's entire brain — and can operate independently of the brain.

science.question: Which recently discovered type of brain cell, reported in 2024-2025 studies, combines properties of both neurons and astrocytes?
science.fact: These hybrid cells release neurotransmitters like neurons but have astrocyte structure, blurring a century-old classification boundary.

science.question: What surprising phenomenon occurs in the brain during general anesthesia that contradicts the assumption of total neural silence?
options: Neurons fire in highly organized slow oscillations
The brain generates gamma waves stronger than during waking
Synaptic connections permanently dissolve and reform
science.fact: Under anesthesia, neurons aren't silent — they fire in powerful synchronized slow waves that block information integration.

history.question: During the Cold War, what was the name of the secret Soviet city that produced plutonium and didn't appear on any maps until 1994?
history.fact: Chelyabinsk-65 housed 80,000+ residents who were forbidden from telling relatives where they lived.

history.question: True or False: During the Cold War, the US military seriously planned to detonate a nuclear bomb on the Moon to intimidate the Soviet Union.
options: It was a Soviet plan, not American
It was a joint US-UK plan
history.fact: Project A119 was real — a young Carl Sagan was hired to study the dust cloud's visibility from Earth.

history.question: Which Cold War-era hotline between Washington and Moscow originally used teletype machines rather than telephones?
history.fact: The famous 'red phone' was never actually a phone — leaders feared voice calls could cause misunderstandings.

history.question: In 2025, declassified British archives revealed that MI6 ran a Cold War propaganda operation using which unexpected cultural medium in Eastern Europe?
history.fact: MI6 secretly funded modern art exhibitions to contrast Western creative freedom with Soviet artistic censorship.

history.question: Which ancient Chinese invention, dating to around 200 BCE, was originally used for divination before its navigational potential was realized?
history.fact: Early Chinese compasses were called 'south-pointing spoons' and were used in feng shui, not sailing.

history.question: How many terracotta warriors are estimated to be buried in the mausoleum complex of Qin Shi Huang?
history.fact: Each warrior has a unique face — researchers believe they were modeled after real individual soldiers.

history.question: During the Warring States period, which ancient Chinese text advocated deception and espionage as essential military tools centuries before Machiavelli?
history.fact: Sun Tzu devoted an entire chapter to spies, classifying five types including 'doomed spies' sent with false information.

history.question: Which cosmonaut became the first person to conduct a spacewalk, nearly dying when his spacesuit inflated uncontrollably in the vacuum?
history.fact: Leonov had to dangerously bleed air from his suit to squeeze back through the airlock — barely surviving.

history.question: What was the name of NASA's first space station, which unexpectedly fell back to Earth scattering debris across Western Australia in 1979?
history.fact: The Shire of Esperance in Australia fined NASA $400 for littering — the fine went unpaid for 30 years.

history.question: Who was the last human to walk on the Moon, doing so in December 1972 during Apollo 17?
history.fact: Cernan traced his daughter's initials in the lunar dust — they'll likely remain there for millions of years.

popculture.question: Which film holds the record for winning the most Oscars without winning Best Picture?
popculture.fact: Cabaret won 8 Oscars in 1973 but lost Best Picture to The Godfather—the biggest such split ever.

popculture.question: At the 2025 Academy Awards, which film won Best Animated Feature?
popculture.fact: Flow was a Latvian film with no dialogue, making it one of the most unconventional animated Oscar winners.

popculture.question: Who is the youngest person ever to win a competitive Academy Award?
popculture.fact: Tatum O'Neal was just 10 years old when she won Best Supporting Actress for Paper Moon in 1974.

popculture.question: True or False: The envelope mix-up at the 2017 Oscars happened because PricewaterhouseCoopers accountants were tweeting backstage.
options: It was a planned publicity stunt
popculture.fact: Brian Cullinan was distracted taking photos of Emma Stone backstage and handed out the wrong envelope.

popculture.question: Which 2000s reality TV show coined the catchphrase 'You're fired!'?
popculture.fact: The phrase became so iconic that Donald Trump attempted to trademark it, though the application faced challenges.

popculture.question: What was the original name of the social media platform that launched at Harvard in 2004 before becoming Facebook?
popculture.fact: TheFacebook.com required a .edu email to join and didn't drop the 'The' until 2005 after buying facebook.com.

popculture.question: Which 2000s animated film features a fish named Nemo but was accused of plagiarizing a 1990s French children's book called 'Pierrot le Poisson-Clown'?
popculture.fact: French author Franck Le Calvez sued Disney-Pixar claiming Finding Nemo copied his 2002 book about a clownfish.

popculture.question: In the 1985 film 'The Breakfast Club,' what item does Bender hide in his pants to smuggle past the principal?
popculture.fact: Bender taped the bag of marijuana to his thigh—John Hughes originally wrote the scene with a harder drug.

popculture.question: Which 1980s one-hit-wonder band performed 'Take On Me,' which holds the record for one of the longest gaps between original release and reaching 1 billion YouTube views?
popculture.fact: The iconic pencil-sketch music video initially flopped in 1984; it was re-shot and became a smash hit in 1985.

popculture.question: How many total viewers watched the final episode of M*A*S*H in 1983, making it the most-watched broadcast in US history at that time?
popculture.fact: So many viewers flushed toilets simultaneously after the finale that New York City water pressure temporarily dropped.

geography.question: Which Asian country contains the world's largest lake by area that lies entirely within one nation?
geography.fact: Lake Balkhash in Kazakhstan is over 16,000 km² — and uniquely, half is freshwater and half is saline.

geography.question: True or False: Mount Everest's summit is NOT the point on Earth's surface farthest from its center.
geography.fact: Ecuador's Mount Chimborazo claims that title because Earth bulges at the equator.

geography.question: In 2025, which Southeast Asian country officially opened its first high-speed railway line, connecting its capital to a major Chinese border city?
geography.fact: The Laos-China Railway runs from Vientiane to Boten, transforming landlocked Laos into a land-linked nation.

geography.question: The Thar Desert straddles the border of India and which other country?
geography.fact: The Thar is the world's most densely populated desert, home to over 80 million people across both nations.

geography.question: Which two European countries share the shortest international land border, measuring just 85 meters?
geography.fact: The border between Spain and the British Overseas Territory of Gibraltar is essentially one fence crossing.

geography.question: How many countries share a land border with Germany?
geography.fact: Germany's nine neighbors are Denmark, Poland, Czechia, Austria, Switzerland, France, Luxembourg, Belgium, and the Netherlands.

geography.question: Uzbekistan and which other country are the only two doubly landlocked countries in the world (surrounded entirely by landlocked countries)?
geography.fact: Tiny Liechtenstein is surrounded by Austria and Switzerland — both landlocked — making it doubly landlocked.

geography.question: Which European microstate has no airport, no railway, and no port, yet has the world's highest GDP per capita?
geography.fact: Monaco relies entirely on France's infrastructure for air and rail access despite being fabulously wealthy.

geography.question: What is Europe's least-visited sovereign country by international tourist arrivals?
geography.fact: Despite being surrounded by Italy, San Marino receives fewer counted overnight tourists than almost any European state.

geography.question: Which European country has more than 50% of its total land area below sea level?
geography.fact: About 26% of the Netherlands is literally below sea level, and roughly 60% is flood-vulnerable without dikes.

sports.question: Which boxing legend retired with a professional record of 49-0, a feat later matched by Floyd Mayweather Jr.?
sports.fact: Marciano is the only heavyweight champion to retire completely undefeated across his entire career.

sports.question: True or False: Lennox Lewis is the last undisputed heavyweight boxing champion, having unified all major belts in 1999.
sports.fact: No heavyweight has held all four major belts simultaneously since Lewis, making it a 25+ year drought.

sports.question: Which boxing great fought in an infamous bout called 'The No Mas Fight' when his opponent quit on the stool?
options: Sugar Ray Leonard vs. Roberto Durán
sports.fact: Durán allegedly said 'No más' in round 8, though he later claimed he said he had stomach cramps.

sports.question: In 2025, which undefeated boxer from Uzbekistan won a unified super-middleweight world title, drawing comparisons to Canelo Álvarez?
sports.fact: Akhmadaliev became one of the few Uzbek fighters to hold multiple major world titles simultaneously.

sports.question: How many Olympic gold medals did Michael Phelps win in the single 2008 Beijing Olympics?
sports.fact: Phelps broke Mark Spitz's 36-year record of 7 golds at a single Games by winning all 8 of his events.

sports.question: Which country has historically dominated Olympic water polo, winning 9 gold medals — more than any other nation?
sports.fact: Hungary once won water polo gold at three consecutive Olympics (2000–2008) with largely the same roster.

sports.question: What unusual swimming stroke was originally used by breaststroke competitors before being banned and reclassified as its own event?
sports.fact: Butterfly emerged as a breaststroke loophole — swimmers used an over-the-water arm recovery that was technically legal.

sports.question: Which country hosted and won the very first FIFA World Cup in 1930?
sports.fact: Only 13 teams participated, and several European nations boycotted due to the long sea voyage to Montevideo.

sports.question: Who is the only player to have scored a hat-trick in a FIFA World Cup final?
sports.fact: Hurst's second goal in the 1966 final remains controversial — many believe the ball never fully crossed the line.

sports.question: Which nation qualified for the 2026 FIFA World Cup for the first time ever after the tournament expanded to 48 teams?
sports.fact: Indonesia's qualification ended a 86-year wait since their only prior appearance as the Dutch East Indies in 1938.

food.question: Which fast food chain originally sold its signature item for just 5 cents when it opened in 1921, making it arguably America's first fast food chain?
food.fact: White Castle predates McDonald's by nearly 30 years and pioneered the standardized fast food model.

food.question: True or False: McDonald's once created a bubble gum-flavored broccoli to get kids to eat healthier.
options: It was strawberry-flavored, not bubble gum
food.fact: CEO Don Thompson confirmed the bizarre experiment, but kids found the taste confusing so it was scrapped.

food.question: In 2025, which major fast food chain made headlines by launching its first fully autonomous AI-operated drive-through locations in the U.S.?
food.fact: Taco Bell partnered with AI voice-ordering tech, expanding automated drive-throughs across multiple states in 2025.

food.question: What was Subway's original name when it opened its first sandwich shop in Bridgeport, Connecticut in 1965?
food.fact: Co-founder Fred DeLuca opened 'Pete's Super Submarines' with a $1,000 loan from family friend Peter Buck.

food.question: In Sardinia, what illegal cheese is intentionally infested with live insect larvae and considered a traditional delicacy?
food.fact: The cheese fly larvae can jump up to 15 centimeters when disturbed, so diners sometimes shield their eyes.

food.question: Which Southeast Asian fruit is so pungent that it's banned from many hotels and public transit systems?
food.fact: Durian's smell has been compared to turpentine and gym socks, yet its custard-like flesh is beloved by millions.

food.question: In Japan, 'shirako' is a prized seasonal delicacy served raw or lightly cooked. What exactly is it?
food.fact: Shirako literally means 'white children' and is typically harvested from cod, anglerfish, or pufferfish.

food.question: Which spice was once so valuable that the Dutch traded the entire island of Manhattan to the British in exchange for control of a tiny island that produced it?
food.fact: The 1667 Treaty of Breda swapped Manhattan for Run Island — a nutmeg-producing speck in Indonesia.

food.question: How many crocus flowers are typically needed to produce a single pound of saffron spice?
food.fact: Each crocus flower yields only three tiny stigma threads, making saffron more expensive than gold by weight.

food.question: Which common herb contains a compound called myristicin that, in extremely large doses, can cause hallucinations and toxicity?
food.fact: Parsley's essential oil contains myristicin — the same psychoactive compound found in much higher concentrations in nutmeg.

music.question: Which singer-songwriter famously wrote 'Hallelujah' but initially had it rejected by his record label, with the album shelved for years?
music.fact: Cohen's label boss reportedly asked, 'Leonard, we know you're great, but we don't know if you're any good.'

music.question: Singer-songwriter Adrianne Lenker of Big Thief released which critically acclaimed solo album in 2025?
music.fact: Lenker recorded much of the album in a remote cabin, continuing her tradition of isolated recording sessions.

music.question: True or False: Joni Mitchell's 'Big Yellow Taxi' was initially a commercial flop in the United States, failing to crack the Top 50.
options: It was never released as a single
music.fact: The song only reached #67 in the US despite becoming one of the most recognized songs of all time.

music.question: Which singer-songwriter wrote 'The First Time Ever I Saw Your Face' in 1957, decades before Roberta Flack's hit version?
music.fact: MacColl wrote it over a phone call to his lover Peggy Seeger, composing each verse on successive nights.

music.question: Which jazz pianist was known for getting up mid-performance to dance beside the piano, and recorded the iconic 'Moanin'' with Art Blakey?
music.fact: Timmons composed 'Moanin'' at just 23 years old, and it became a hard bop anthem almost overnight.

music.question: What unusual instrument did blues legend Robert Johnson reportedly tune using a technique called 'Spanish tuning'?
music.fact: Open G tuning was called 'Spanish' by Delta blues players and was key to Johnson's haunting slide sound.

music.question: Which jazz standard was originally written in 1930 but became controversial because its verse references 'gallant sons of little brown brothers'?
music.fact: Cole Porter's 'Love for Sale' was banned from radio airplay for its frank depiction of prostitution.

music.question: Dexys Midnight Runners are famous for 'Come On Eileen,' but what was the group's musical style primarily rooted in?
music.fact: Leader Kevin Rowland enforced strict dress codes on band members, firing anyone who broke the image rules.

music.question: Which one-hit wonder's 'Take On Me' failed on its first release in 1984, only becoming a global smash after a rerecorded version and iconic video in 1985?
music.fact: The original 1984 version peaked at #137 in the UK before the pencil-sketch video made it a phenomenon.

music.question: Who performed the 1996 one-hit wonder 'Macarena,' which spent 14 weeks at #1 on the Billboard Hot 100?
music.fact: The duo were in their 50s when 'Macarena' became the biggest hit, making them among the oldest chart-toppers ever.

technology.question: What was the name of the NSA surveillance program revealed by Edward Snowden that collected bulk phone metadata from millions of Americans?
technology.fact: The program collected call records of virtually every American, yet the NSA admitted it never uniquely cracked a single terror plot.

technology.question: In 2025, which major browser vendor announced it would disable uBlock Origin's Manifest V2 extensions by default, sparking a cybersecurity debate?
technology.fact: Chrome's Manifest V3 transition limited ad-blocker capabilities, pushing many security-conscious users to switch browsers.

technology.question: True or False: The infamous Stuxnet worm that targeted Iranian centrifuges could only spread via USB drives and never used the internet.
options: False — it also spread via local networks and zero-day Windows exploits
False — it spread only via email attachments
False — it spread exclusively over the internet
technology.fact: Stuxnet exploited four separate Windows zero-day vulnerabilities simultaneously — an unprecedented level of sophistication for malware.

technology.question: Which cybersecurity technique deliberately places fake data or decoy systems to detect and mislead attackers?
technology.fact: The first honeypot concept dates to 1989 — Clifford Stoll used fake files to track a KGB-linked hacker in 'The Cuckoo's Egg.'

technology.question: What is the term for AI-generated outputs that sound confident and plausible but are factually incorrect or fabricated?
technology.fact: In 2023, a lawyer submitted a court brief with fake case citations entirely hallucinated by ChatGPT — the judge was not amused.

technology.question: Which AI architecture, introduced in a 2017 paper titled 'Attention Is All You Need,' underpins most modern large language models?
technology.fact: All eight authors of that landmark paper eventually left Google — several founded competing AI startups worth billions.

technology.question: In early 2025, which company released an open-weight AI model that rivaled GPT-4 performance while being trainable at a fraction of the cost?
technology.fact: DeepSeek's R1 model reportedly cost under $6 million to train, shocking an industry accustomed to hundred-million-dollar training budgets.

technology.question: Which tech billionaire sold his first company, Zip2, for approximately $307 million in 1999 before going on to co-found PayPal?
technology.fact: Musk personally earned $22 million from the Zip2 sale and immediately poured almost all of it into his next startup, X.com.

technology.question: Who became the world's youngest self-made billionaire in tech history at age 23, a record that still stands?
technology.fact: Zuckerberg became a billionaire in 2008 — Bill Gates didn't reach that milestone until age 31 despite starting Microsoft earlier.

technology.question: Tech billionaire Larry Ellison, co-founder of Oracle, owns approximately 98% of which Hawaiian island?
technology.fact: Ellison bought Lanai in 2012 for roughly $300 million and has since built sustainable farms and luxury resorts on the island.

nature.question: Which prehistoric shark, often called the largest fish ever, had teeth that were actually smaller and more numerous than Megalodon's?
nature.fact: Leedsichthys was a filter feeder up to 16 meters long — it had no need for giant teeth at all!

nature.question: True or False: Tyrannosaurus rex lived closer in time to humans than to Stegosaurus.
options: They all overlapped within 50 million years
nature.fact: About 80 million years separated Stegosaurus and T. rex, but only 66 million years separate T. rex from us.

nature.question: In 2025, researchers announced the discovery of remarkably preserved stomach contents in which iconic extinct bird, revealing its last meal?
nature.fact: Preserved moa gizzard contents showed seeds from plants now also extinct — a lost ecological partnership.

nature.question: What was unusual about the giant ground sloth Mylodon's skin that helped protect it from predators?
options: It was venomous to the touch
It contained embedded bony nodules called osteoderms
It was covered in sharp quills
nature.fact: Mylodon had thousands of pebble-like bones embedded in its skin, creating natural chainmail armor.

nature.question: Which coral reef organism can actually survive being completely bleached by switching to a predatory feeding mode instead of relying on symbiotic algae?
nature.fact: Mushroom corals can catch zooplankton with their tentacles, surviving months without their symbiotic algae.

nature.question: How does the pistol shrimp stun its prey near coral reefs?
options: Snapping a claw to create a cavitation bubble hotter than the sun's surface
Releasing concentrated venom into the water
nature.fact: The collapsing cavitation bubble briefly reaches temperatures of about 4,700°C — nearly as hot as the sun's surface.

nature.question: What percentage of the ocean floor do coral reefs cover, despite supporting roughly 25% of all marine species?
nature.fact: Coral reefs cover just 0.1% of the ocean floor yet harbor a quarter of all known marine species.

nature.question: Which great ape has been documented using medicinal plants to treat wounds by chewing leaves and applying them as a poultice?
nature.fact: A Sumatran orangutan was filmed in 2024 treating a facial wound with a medicinal plant called Akar Kuning.

nature.question: Bonobos are known for resolving conflicts differently than chimpanzees. What is their primary conflict-resolution strategy?
nature.fact: Bonobos use sexual behavior to ease nearly every type of social tension — even disputes over food.

nature.question: Which primate has the highest brain-to-body-size ratio of any non-human primate, surprisingly beating all great apes?
nature.fact: Capuchins have a brain-to-body ratio comparable to early hominids and are expert tool users in the wild.

film.question: In Ridley Scott's 'Alien' (1979), what unusual substance was used to create the alien egg's interior membrane effects?
options: Cattle intestines and real sheep kidneys
film.fact: Real organic animal parts were used inside the alien eggs to create the disturbing, living texture.

film.question: Which 2025 sci-fi film featured a generation ship where the AI secretly edited passengers' memories during cryosleep?
film.fact: Mickey 17, based on Edward Ashton's novel, was directed by Bong Joon-ho and starred Robert Pattinson.

film.question: True or False: The sound design for the lightsaber hum in Star Wars was created partly from interference on a TV set picked up by a microphone.
options: It came from a motorcycle engine recording
film.fact: Ben Burtt combined a film projector motor's hum with TV set interference to create the iconic sound.

film.question: In 'Blade Runner 2049,' how many years after the original 1982 film is the story set?
film.fact: The original is set in 2019 and the sequel in 2049—a 30-year gap in-universe but titled '2049.'

film.question: Which Iranian film won the Palme d'Or at Cannes but was initially banned in its home country?
film.fact: Abbas Kiarostami's 'Taste of Cherry' (1997) won the Palme d'Or but faced censorship issues in Iran.

film.question: Who directed the 2004 Thai martial arts film 'Ong-Bak: Muay Thai Warrior' that used no CGI or wire work for stunts?
film.fact: Tony Jaa performed all stunts himself with no wires, CGI, or stunt doubles—every hit was real.

film.question: Which country's film industry is known as 'Nollywood' and is the second-largest by volume of films produced annually?
film.fact: Nigeria's Nollywood produces roughly 2,500 films per year, surpassing Hollywood's annual output by volume.

film.question: Who is the youngest person ever to receive an Academy Award nomination for Best Director?
film.fact: John Singleton was just 24 when nominated for 'Boyz n the Hood' (1991)—a record that still stands.

film.question: How many consecutive Academy Awards ceremonies did Walt Disney win at least one Oscar, setting an all-time record streak?
film.fact: Disney won Oscars at eight consecutive ceremonies from 1932–1940, a streak never matched by anyone.

film.question: Which film holds the record for the most Oscar nominations without winning a single award?
options: Both The Turning Point and The Color Purple
film.fact: Both films received 11 nominations each and won zero—the most snubbed films in Oscar history.

politics.question: Which country became the last in the world to grant women the right to vote in national elections, doing so only in 2015?
politics.fact: Saudi women could first vote in 2015 municipal elections, though national elections remain limited for everyone.

politics.question: True or False: In Australia, you can be fined for NOT voting in federal elections.
politics.fact: Australia has enforced compulsory voting since 1924, with fines currently around AUD $20-$80 for non-voters.

politics.question: Which European country used a citizens' assembly of randomly selected people to draft its 2012 constitutional reform proposal?
politics.fact: Iceland's crowdsourced constitution was drafted by 25 ordinary citizens but was never formally adopted by parliament.

politics.question: In 2025, which Pacific island nation held a controversial referendum on its political status, potentially altering its free association agreement with the United States?
politics.fact: Palau's compact of free association renegotiations drew global attention amid growing US-China competition in the Pacific.

politics.question: The 1932 Constitutionalist Revolution was the last major armed civil conflict in which country's history?
politics.fact: São Paulo state revolted against Getúlio Vargas's federal government, losing militarily but winning constitutionally by 1934.

politics.question: Which uprising saw Paris workers govern the city for 72 days before being violently suppressed in 'Bloody Week' of 1871?
politics.fact: The Commune introduced radical policies like separating church and state 34 years before France officially did.

politics.question: During the 1986 People Power Revolution in the Philippines, which military leader's defection was the critical turning point against Marcos?
politics.fact: Defense Minister Enrile and Vice Chief of Staff Ramos defected together, triggering the massive civilian uprising.

politics.question: Which small EU member state held the rotating presidency of the Council of the European Union in the first half of 2025?
politics.fact: Poland's presidency focused on European security and defense amid the ongoing war in Ukraine.

politics.question: How many seats does the European Parliament currently have after the 2024 elections?
politics.fact: The 2024 elections expanded the Parliament from 705 to 720 seats to reflect demographic changes across member states.

politics.question: Which EU mechanism, triggered for the first time against Poland in 2017, allows the bloc to address threats to the rule of law in member states?
politics.fact: Article 7 is nicknamed the 'nuclear option' but has never reached its final stage of suspending voting rights.
